pattern: Fixing how many of the not-yet-complete gardens get finished off (always the ones closest to target, since that is cheapest) turns the leftover budget question into a single binary search for the highest floor you can raise the remaining gardens' minimum to.
hints: Gardens already at or above target are free complete gardens and never change; focus only on the gardens still below target, sorted by how many flowers they still need.

If you decide exactly r of the below-target gardens will become complete, the cheapest way is always to complete the r gardens that are closest to target already -- that leaves the most budget for the rest.

For a fixed choice of r, whatever flowers are left over should all go toward raising the minimum of the remaining (still incomplete) gardens as high as possible; binary search that ceiling using prefix sums, try every value of r, and keep the best total beauty.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, best plan leaves one garden incomplete
statement example, completing everything would be worse than leaving some incomplete
single garden already complete, no incomplete gardens ever
single garden far below target with a huge new-flowers budget
budget is exactly enough to complete every garden, but that is not optimal
zero budget to plant anything, answer depends only on current counts
all gardens already complete, partial term must stay zero
partial is worth far more than full, favors raising the minimum over completing gardens

stress.description: random garden counts against random targets, budgets, and reward weights
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, min(max(size, 1), 200))
    target = rng.randint(1, 50)
    flowers = [rng.randint(1, target + 20) for _ in range(n)]
    new_flowers = rng.randint(0, n * target + 50)
    full = rng.randint(1, 100)
    partial = rng.randint(1, 100)
    return [flowers, new_flowers, target, full, partial]
